C[Mef: Ignoring R3 is normally not suggested (since it is basically a contact move), but I think the real problem black wants to look at is staying connected with his N4 /Q4 stones.  O3 basically forces white to play P4 (which cuts black in two).
Mef: Instead black may want to play P4 or O4 - keep all his stones as once strong group
Mef: C13 is an empty triangle, an inefficient shape.   Black's group would be stronger if this was at B13 (a more efficient shape) also H12 is big because it keeps white split apart.

C[Mef: F4 is a little too easy on white.  Black's stones are stronger than white's here, so black wants to cut!  E6 or F6 both leave white in a tough spot because he is split in two
Mef: Also F8 should be G8 - this protects the eye at E7 while making space for a second one at F8
Mef: Black should be braver than to play A5 (=
Mef: E12 at G12 would capture the white stones in a net
Mef: B12 is an example of an inefficient shape called the ""empty triangle.""  Black should try to avoid this shape and instead play B13.  This protects the group and makes more space for eyes.
Mef: E14 is another empty triangle )=
Mef: Again it's an inefficient shape, and white gets to use it as a chance to seal black in.
Mef: Because of the snapback threat, G14 is actually a bit of an overplay for white, though it is a tough fight.

Mef: What is really crazy is that even if white sees the snapback, at Move 75 black can play G13 - white H12 (avoid snapback) - black H13! This feels like an overplay because there is the cutting point black needs to fix, but black is ok. If white cuts right now at F13, then black J12, white H11, black J11 white H10, black H9 is a ladder that runs into black's N4 stone so black wins it!  Amazing how just 1 stone way across the board can make a difference!  Now after H13 white J12 black has to fix the cutting point....
